sentences.text: My name is Leon Scher.
S-C-H-E-R.
At birth was Spirer, S-P-I-E-R.
Spirer.
R-E-R.
R-E-R.
My birthday is March 3rd, 1915.
Today I'm 81.
I was born in Czechoslovakia.
The city was Rechlitz and the state was Reichenberg.
Reichenberg.
Rechlitz.
R-E-C-H-L-I-T-Z.
My father was Tobias Spirer.
My mother, the maiden name was Malka Neiss, S-- E-I-S-S, Neiss.
And--
They were like-- my father, I never know my father.
And my father didn't know me.
This was in the First World War.
My father was taken in the Polish Army in the war and he got killed in the war.
He never know me and I didn't know my father.
So my mother before-- when they took my father to the Army, she went to Czechoslovakia with four children.
And there was other people from the family where they went.
They were afraid to be in Poland, where the-- Why was that?
They were afraid because in the war, there was a skirmish also with the Russians and the Cossacks.
And they were right away-- the Jews, they were against Jews and killing also Jews.
So my mother was afraid with children, so she went to Czechoslovakia.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after the war, my mother came back.
He was killed approximately 1915 or 1916.
Was never-- they never found his body.
And he never came back, so we know that he was killed.
So--
Before she lived on a colony, in a village [INAUDIBLE], not far from the county of Jaworow.
Now, on that colony, we had a little farm.
We had three acres of land.
And we have houses and all kind of-- you know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Also, we had an acre of forest to need for the winter, keeping warm the house and cooking and all of that stuff.
And also for the cows, some land to feed the cows.
So we had five acres of land.
Now, when my mother came back, all our property was burned completely.
Oh, god, I don't know, a year and a half, two years, I think, 'til it got quiet.
Then she came back and everything was burned so we didn't have anywhere to live.
So my grandma lived about two miles away.
So we--
My grandmother's name was Ester.
Because my grandfather was very young-- he died very young, my grandfather, died very young.
I never know my grandfather either.
So we lived together and they had a big house.
They have like a liquor store and a big house and a bigger farm, so we lived there with my grandma and also an aunt and an uncle.

So we lived there, but then with five children, it was really hard to live together, so my mother was thinking to build a house, but in a difference place, closer to my grandma, like a half a mile away.
So we had to give somebody land where we had and take a small space just to build a house and have a little garden.
And that's what happened.
So my grandfather from my mother's father came from another village, [INAUDIBLE].
He came and he stayed with us and he start to build a house for us.
He was like, knowing in building.
And then he had to help, so he got from our-- from our forest be brought wood and he chiseled and he did it with help and he built a house.
Now, the house was not-- it was very a poor house, because one room, we had a big room.
Then we had a foyer and we had also another big room for storage for food, and then a cellar for milk and all that.
We had two cows.
We had chickens, everything what we need so we could live quietly.
I had two brothers, two sisters, two older sisters and two older brothers.
My older sister was Sarah.
She was born 1908.
My other sister, the second sister, was Perl.
She was born 1909.
Then my brother, my older brother, was born 1912.
My second brother was born 1914.
I was born 1915 in Czechoslovakia.
I was the youngest.
Well, I was very young and they were older, much older for me.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had always good relations, because we had to respect our mother.
We didn't have no father, so she was the father and the mother.
So always you were very respectable.
Mother was very religious.
Also my grandfather was very religious, so you have to respect.
It was really not to do things, nothing wrong, just everything has to be right, not to disturb the mother, not to get her nervous.
So we had a discipline by ourself.
You made [INAUDIBLE].
And she was a very good mother, Nice looking, beautiful lady.
I got a picture from my-- my mother and father when she sent-- when she got married, she sent to the United States to my aunt and uncles.
So I found it here after 1949 when I came here and found those pictures what my aunt gave me.
All the pictures from my grandma I got pictures, and cousins.
I mean we were Orthodox Jews, not really fanatic.
Oh, my grandfather, he was very religious.
But the kids, the grandchildren, starting to get even a little more modern.
But we were going to shul every Saturday.
We were [INAUDIBLE] put on tefillin, you know, and-- whenever you were home.
Later when we were out of home was a different story, so you also then Saturdays to shul.
But-- and-- we-- we were quite, but not over-done, not over-done religious.
Oh yes, right by the wedding she had a wig.
Had her hair before the wedding, he shared a wig to the wedding.
Religious education, we didn't go like actually to a regular like a cheder, a Jewish school, because my mother couldn't afford to send us.
And in the village, once we had somebody come and Hebrew teach and learn us, a rabbi to learn us, but very seldom.
But we had an uncle, my mother's father.
He was a very intelligent, highly intelligent-- all languages.
He speaked a lot of languages, even Arabic.
And he was-- he had a lot of-- a lot of thinking himself.
But it's really a genius.
He played violin, beautiful violin.
And he played chess, the best chess player.
And-- and also a artist, artistically, you know, painting what he teached us.
And he teached us everything from [NON-ENGLISH] to Russia.
And not [NON-ENGLISH].
He don't believe so much a [NON-ENGLISH], just [NON-ENGLISH].
His-- his favorite was [NON-ENGLISH], all the books.
He was really a genius.
He was learning later.
In Krakow, he was learning with people after shul.
At night, they come, 300, 400 people.
And he was learning with them, you know, from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the-- from the Shabbas, Saturday's reading of the Torah, all kind of-- very intelligent person.
So we learned a little bit, not too much.
But whatever he learned with us, it stick to our heads.
And we really-- oh, my brother was reading the Torah.
And everybody was learning read and write Jewish.
And that was-- So, could you describe further when he came to teach you Jewish studies, what-- how did you spend the time, what it was like?
Oh, we were spending the time-- was busy time, actually.
First I went to school two years when I was a little kid, to Ukrainian school, because this belonged to Ukraine.
But was Poland and Ukrainian.
Ukrainians were there.
So I went two years to a Ukrainian school.
And then the other four years, four and a half years, I went in a colony, in a polish colony what they call [NON-ENGLISH], Polish colony.
And you had a teacher, she was teaching only Polish.
And I had a lot of Polish friends, what my Mother and Father were close neighbors, Polish neighbors.
So I was going like two miles for that Polish school.
But I learned.
I learned.
I know very good Polish, [INAUDIBLE] Polish.
I speak Polish.
I also speak Ukrainian.
And later when the Russian came, I spoke Russian, learned.
What was your favorite subject at school?
In school was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no.
[INAUDIBLE] say [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] No.
I don't know how to explain it.
That's geography.
Geography, to know the rivers in the-- in the-- going in from one to another and all kind of this subject, I was very interested.
Yes.
This uncle, yes.
Yes.
Even he was not every year, but he came from time to time.
He came.
And he was really like a father.
His name was Izchok Neiss.
This is my mother's brother.
He was my mother's middle brother, because there were an older-- the oldest was a sister.
My mother's oldest sister was Sarah.
She had 12 children.
We had a big family.
And my other uncle was Abraham Neiss.
He lived in another city, [INAUDIBLE], the city.
And he had two-- three children.
And this is-- the third one was my uncle was the third one.
Then I had another uncle, Jakob, Jakob.
And my mother.
And then after my mother was another uncle, Majlech, Uncle Majlech Neiss.
But my cousin, their-- one of his daughter survived, my first cousin.
She survived.
She worked in Germany on papers which she got from somebody from a Polish bureau.
And she got papers and she was not-- she was taken to Germany like other people, Polish people taken in to-- to work in Germany.
She was in Germany in [GERMAN].
Oh, we dressed-- we dressed pretty nice.
We all dressed very nice, very nice.
First of all, we had family in the United States, so they sent packages, all kind of clothes for the girls, for the whole family.
And they sent packages for the men, suits and all kind of thing.
And later, when we grow up, we start working for ourselves.
I used to go out and do business, you know, with Gentile people, selling, buying, and do business.
So I clothed myself.
I really clothed myself very good.
Oh, to shul, it was always very, very proper, like the shul, always in my jacket and a tie and dress real nice.
Oh, we have a very good, very good-- we communicate with the Gentile people, with the Ukrainian very good.
They respected my mother, first of all.
My mother was a widow, so they all respected.
Even when we built the house in [INAUDIBLE], a lot of Gentile people gave stuff, whatever they could help, materials, straw, and all kind of things, whatever you need to build a house.
They were very, very nice to us.
But then my mother are very good.
She also knows all about-- she helped Ukrainian people with kids.
They had always a lot of kids and some of them were sick, you know.
So she helped them.
And she-- she was putting down those-- in Europe, they put down-- you have a cold or whatever, so you put on such a glass with spirits.
You put it on on the body.
And this cleared the-- the sickness.
And also, they had some kind of worms where they put it on when you have pneumonia.
She also-- we had this, what she was going-- anybody they call, they came, they call my mother.
And she came and she know how to do that, to put on the body.
They soak out the blood and that helped for the pneumonia.
That's what the old-- the old doctors.
So she was an old doctor like that.
And they all respected her.
Even the priest in the city, he always respect my mother very good.
He always used to buy cigarettes, specially only by us, because she was a widow, so he stopped.
He bought two cartons, three cartons of cigarettes.
He said, you can't buy no place else, only from my mother.
Very nice.
He was very nice.
But later on, it started getting the anti-Semitism.
How did your mother make a living?
Oh, made a living.
We had our own food.
We went to the-- we went to the-- to the field.
And you had somebody go in with, you know, making the land, you know, grow everything to help, us, you know, we paid them, you know.
And we were going also we worked.
We cleaned the grass out, whatever.
It's no good.
And we did a lot of work.
Then we have a garden.
We have a nice garden.
I planted also.
I like to work in the garden very much.
I even planted-- my grandfather, we plant like apple trees and pears and all kinds of fruit, cherries.
We plant in the front of the garden trees.
And later, they grow up.
We had our own-- our own fruit we had.
We had two cows.
We had chickens.
We had-- we had what-- even my mother used to bring two, three times a year to the city of Krakow.
Yes.
There were a lot of poor people, lot of poor people.
And she know already all the poor people, so we used to hire somebody.
We had a special-- one man what we hired.
And when she packed up packages, you know for each one, what to do and how-- how many children they have, and bring packages with food.
This was two, three times a year.
Yeah.
That's what-- and we didn't live high.
We weren't rich, but we weren't hungry.
We weren't hungry.
At home, Yiddish.
At home, Yiddish.
Outside, we speak Ukrainian, Polish.
We speak--
For reading, books?
Oh, I liked stories, all kind.
You had going in the newspaper, the Jewish newspapers, they call it [NON-ENGLISH], between Rome and Jerusalem, the war.
And it was a story, every-- every week a story continuing.
I liked to-- to read this.
And then I read Polish books about Sherlock Holmes.
I loved his story, the English Sherlock Holmes, all kind of story.
He's a detective, yeah.
What kind of detective he was, and-- I was really in-- in love with his stories, Sherlock Holmes.
I read Jewish books, Jewish stories.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
You had Jewish-- Jewish-- yes.
We had Jewish newspapers.
Jewish community, there was not much in the village.
Like our village, there was my grandma and uncles and children.
Then further on another kilometer, there was my uncle, my father's brother, with a wife and children.
No.
Before that was my grandma's brother with a big family, a very big family.
And he was a farmer also.
He had like 16 acres, 17 acres.
We had five acres.
He had-- you know.
And my grandma also had 10 acres, 12 acres of land.
And going further, there was a lot of cousins, cousins.
And there was another family, so we were 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 7 families.
So we had by my uncle, we go to daven every Saturday, Friday night.
We go to the shul.
[INAUDIBLE] And there was a Sefer Torah, scroll, where my uncle was reading the Torah.
My brother was reading the Torah.
And that-- that's [INAUDIBLE], because we got a Torah with my uncle sent money, I don't know, $100 or $150 those years-- yeah. $100 to $150 and we bought a Torah scroll.
We bought it.
It was for the mother, in the honor of their mother.
I had two uncles and an aunt.
But one of my uncles was very good up, so he sent the mostly-- he sent the money.
We bought a scroll and this was putting in-- it was sometimes in the winter.
They were davened by my grandma.
She had a big house.
We were davened by my grandma.
And also on the holidays, because to the city was like five, six kilometers to the city, so we didn't go to the city.
So we had our own.
And some people came from other religious-- from other villages to us, to my grandma to daven.
So we had sometime me and 40, 50 people, they come for our [INAUDIBLE], especially for the big holidays, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur and all the other holidays, Sukkot, Shavuot, Pesach.
No.
A [NON-ENGLISH] had to come from the city.
We had a [NON-ENGLISH].
And whatever-- we needed chickens, so my mother took some chickens in a basket and brought it to the city.
We had-- twice a week, they had the people come from the villages, brought out food to the city.
It was a Monday and a Thursday, especially on Thursday.
So we brought some chickens.
My mother came to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And sometimes when we'd have-- I used to buy little calves.
I used to deal with that too.
So brought in the [NON-ENGLISH], brought it in.
And he came and he [INAUDIBLE] me and by other family.
And I used to take the skin off and do the whole thing, you know, pulling down with the arm, the skin goes off.
And then we had all kinds of food.
We had meat and everything, and with meat, other meat, like roasts and all of this.
So we had a little far distance family, he had butcher store in the city.
So he know-- when he was giving to my mother so she don't have to watch him.
He always treat her good, but she knows every piece of meat to tell him what she wants and that's what he had to give her.
Couldn't fool-- sometimes the butcher are not so honest-- who didn't know, but she know.
So that's the way it worked.
Was there any theater in Yiddish at the time?
What?
A seder?
Oh, theater?
No.
Oh, yeah.
We had-- in [INAUDIBLE], we had a theater.
We had a movie house.
Krakow, we didn't have a movie house.
We had sometimes shows, like private shows made from people.
So we went to Krakow to a show.
Otherwise to a movie, we had in [INAUDIBLE], we had a movie house, nice movie house, also, a Jewish theater, very good acting and everything, making like regular theaters, you know.
In Yiddish.
Oh, yes, In Yiddish.
Everything was in Yiddish.
And like they made like the [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] they made.
And they made also-- when Abraham-- when Abraham took his son, you know, for-- to take him.
So they had a show from this.
A lot of other shows, really nice shows, so we went.
But when I was in Krakow, this was already a difference.
In Krakow, we lived in Krakow before the war.
So then already, the Jewish theaters and the-- was really beautiful.
Movies, theaters, everything.
Oh, free time, I was always busy.
I was a little devil.
[LAUGHS] Very busy, always exercise.
A lot of exercise I used to do, jumping and far away and this.
And it came handy when I was in the Polish Army.
It came very handy.
We used to like to ride horses with our neighbors, ride horses, and also work in the field, you know, and go out doing business.

After when I was 13, after my bar mitzvah, I was out from the house already doing business with Gentile people, you know, and taking all kind of foods with big-- lot of wagons and going like to Lemberg was quite a distance, far away.
We used to bring stuff, selling there.
Sometimes you couldn't sell.
Too much vegetables, you couldn't sell, so you just drop it and throw it-- throw it in the river, throw it in the river.
Something was overloaded, ex-- it just happened that everybody that bringed in too much food.
And so much of so much you can sell.
So you couldn't sell the whole thing.
The rest, we'd throw it out.
So some days was good.
Some days was not so good.
Political party, I belonged to a Jewish organization, Akiba.
The name was Akiba.
And we used to come together from all the villages, come together, speeches.
They had a president.
He was a lawyer, forgot already his name.
So in my mind.
I forgot his name.
He was the head-- the president on the Akiba.
Akiba is what-- like-- like the Ben-Gurion, like Ben-Gurion organization.
It was in the middle, you know.
They were not to the left, not to the right, in the middle.
And I was young, at that time.
I was young.
Maybe also my cousins, a lot of cousins, they went on the Hachshara, going to farmers and learning the trade, because they want to go to Israel.
So one of my cousins, she went-- she married and she went with her husband.
She went to Israel about 1923, '25.
And then my other cousin, her brother, also went on Hachshara.
And he went also to France.
He worked in France on a farm.
From there, he went to Jerusalem.
He got killed, 1948 war.
He got killed in Jerusalem.
And we used to come together, you know, have dances and speeches and all kind of work to my family in [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah.
It was a big family day in my mother's family.
We used to go quite a lot.
Hasidic, but not fanatic.
My zaida, maybe, yes.
My mother was already very religious, but not-- modern, very modern.
